summary: This brief post introduces the concept of the 'design payoff line' as part of Fowler's broader Design Stamina Hypothesis. It defines the threshold of functionality below which teams can legitimately trade design quality for faster time to market. The implication is that for very small projects or features, investing in good design may not pay off before delivery. Beyond this line, however, poor design choices accumulate as drag on development speed.
key_points: There exists a threshold of functionality where design quality trade-offs become meaningful
Below the design payoff line, sacrificing design for speed can be a valid choice
This concept is part of the larger Design Stamina Hypothesis framework
The framing suggests that design investment has diminishing returns for very small scopes
money_quotes: The design payoff line is the amount of functionality below which it is possible to trade off design quality for time to market.
key_insight: For sufficiently small projects, the investment in good design may never pay off before delivery, making design shortcuts a rational choice below a certain threshold of functionality.
